result.markdown:
---
title: "Parquet Files and DuckDB"
subtitle: "How {obus} serves 14 million rows in under a second"
date-modified: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
---



This article explains three layers that power `{obus}` under the hood — **Parquet**, **DuckDB**, and **dbplyr** — and why they matter even if you never have to think about them directly.

The short version: the full DATRAS exchange tables (HH, HL, CA) live on a web server as Parquet files. When you call `dr_con()`, a tiny in-process database called DuckDB connects to those files. A third layer, `{dbplyr}`, translates ordinary `{dplyr}` code into SQL so DuckDB can execute it — without ever downloading the parts you don't need. You can switch to a regular R data frame at any point and **the same `{dplyr}` code keeps working unchanged**.

---

## Why not just CSV?

The traditional way to share tabular data is a CSV file. CSV is readable by nearly any tool, which is why it has outlasted every format that tried to replace it. But CSV has three weaknesses that become painful at DATRAS scale:

**No type information.** A CSV file is plain text. When you read it, the parser has to guess whether `"2026"` is a year (integer) or an ID (character). DATRAS has had persistent problems with fields like `StNo` arriving as integer in some national submissions and character in others, causing `bind_rows()` to fail. Every time you read a CSV you re-run the guessing game.

**Reads the whole file.** If you want only the `Survey`, `Year`, and `HaulNumber` columns from a 14-million-row HL file, a CSV reader still reads all 30 columns, then discards the ones you did not ask for.

**No compression awareness.** A plain CSV of the full HL table is multiple gigabytes. The same data in Parquet fits in a fraction of that — the exact numbers are measured in the [Compression](#compression) section below.


Parquet fixes all three

---

## Parquet: the columnar file format

### Columns, not rows

A CSV stores one row at a time:

```
Survey,Year,Quarter,Country,Platform,...
NS-IBTS,2026,1,Scotland,Scotia,...
NS-IBTS,2026,1,Scotland,Scotia,...
```

Parquet stores one **column** at a time: all `Survey` values together, then all `Year` values together, and so on. This sounds like a minor implementation detail, but the consequence is important: if you ask for only `Survey` and `Year`, Parquet reads only those two columns from disk. The other 28 columns are never touched.

### Metadata stored in the file

Every Parquet file carries a **footer** — a compact block of binary metadata at the end of the file. The footer contains:

- column names
- column types (`INT32`, `UTF8`, `DOUBLE`, …)
- number of rows
- row-group boundaries (the file is split into chunks; the footer says where each starts)
- min/max statistics per chunk, per column

When DuckDB first connects to a Parquet file it reads only the footer — a few kilobytes even for a file with millions of rows. From that it knows the schema, the row count, and which chunks it can skip entirely when a filter is applied.

### Strict types

Types are recorded in the file and enforced on read. An `INT32` column comes back as integer, a `UTF8` column comes back as character — no guessing, no surprises. When reading from the XML source (`dr_get(source = "xml")`), the parquet file is not involved at all; types are applied separately by `dr_settypes()` using the `dr_lookup_fields` table as its reference. Either way the result is a correctly typed data frame, but the two paths are independent.

### Compression

Within each column chunk, values are compressed. Parquet supports several algorithms; the obus server files use **Snappy**, a fast algorithm optimised for decompression speed. Similar values compress extremely well: a column that is the string `"NS-IBTS"` repeated 500 000 times compresses to almost nothing. Numeric columns with many repeated values (like `Quarter`, which is only 1–4) shrink similarly.


::: {.cell}

:::


The result, measured directly from the server file and a freshly written CSV of the same data: the full HL table (14,397,334 rows, 30 columns) occupies **97 MB** as Parquet. Writing the identical data as a plain CSV produces a **2.1 GB** file — 22 × larger.

---

## DuckDB: an in-process analytical database

### What it is

[DuckDB](https://duckdb.org) is a relational database engine designed for analytical queries on large tables. Unlike PostgreSQL or MySQL, DuckDB runs **in-process** — it starts up as a library inside your R session, with no separate server to install, configure, or connect to. When you install `{obus}`, DuckDB comes along as an R package dependency and is ready to use immediately. DuckDB can also store tables in its own `.duckdb` file rather than reading from external Parquet files; `{obus}` uses the external-file mode because the data lives on a server, but both modes are available.

### Three ways to get an `hl` object — and why it doesn't matter

Before going further, here are the three ways you might start a DATRAS analysis, and what you end up with:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# 1. Connect to the obus server (lazy — nothing downloaded yet)
hl <- dr_con("HL")

# 2. Connect to a local copy (lazy — identical object to dr_con())
hl <- duckdbfs::open_dataset("~/datras/HL.parquet")

# 3. Import the full table into R memory
hl <- dr_get("HL")
```
:::


**Options 1 and 2 return a lazy DuckDB connection. Option 3 returns a plain R data frame.** These are different objects internally, but from your perspective as a user they behave identically: every `{dplyr}` verb (`filter`, `mutate`, `group_by`, `summarise`, `left_join`, …) and every `dr_add_*()` function in `{obus}` works the same way on all three. You do not need to know or care which one you have.

The rest of this article uses `hl` from `dr_con()`, but every single line of code shown would work unchanged if `hl` had come from `duckdbfs::open_dataset()` or `dr_get()` instead.

### Connecting is nearly instant; importing is not


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
system.time({
  hl <- dr_con("HL")   # lazy connection — reads only the file footer
})
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   user  system elapsed 
  0.425   0.052   0.483 
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
system.time({
  hl_imported <- dr_get("HL")   # full import — all rows into R memory
})
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   user  system elapsed 
  5.920   0.944  16.722 
```


:::
:::


The difference is what has actually happened. `dr_con()` sends an HTTP HEAD request, then tells DuckDB to note the file location and read its footer — a few kilobytes. The 14-million-row table has not moved. `dr_get()` downloads the entire file.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
class(hl)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "tbl_duckdb_connection" "tbl_dbi"               "tbl_sql"              
[4] "tbl_lazy"              "tbl"                  
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
nrow(hl)   # NA — DuckDB has not counted rows yet
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] NA
```


:::
:::


The `NA` row count signals that `hl` is a **promise** of data, not data itself. A query will be needed to make anything happen.

---

## dbplyr: writing dplyr, getting SQL

### The translation layer

`{dbplyr}` is the package that makes the magic happen. It intercepts ordinary `{dplyr}` verbs — `filter()`, `select()`, `mutate()`, `group_by()`, `summarise()`, `left_join()` — and translates them into SQL, which DuckDB can then execute against the Parquet file. The translation goes beyond `{dplyr}`: many base R functions (`mean()`, `sum()`, `is.na()`, `paste()`, `case_when()`, string and date helpers, and more) are also recognised and converted to their SQL equivalents. This is not new: `{dbplyr}` has long been used to connect R to traditional databases such as PostgreSQL, Oracle, and SQL Server — DuckDB with Parquet is simply the most recent addition to that list.

You never have to write a line of SQL. You write `{dplyr}`:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
q <- hl |>
  filter(Survey == "NS-IBTS", Year == 2026, Quarter == 1)
```
:::


What does `q` contain? Still a lazy query. To see the SQL that was built:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
q |> show_query()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
<SQL>
SELECT *
FROM oiqkicywxxllxon
WHERE (Survey = 'NS-IBTS') AND ("Year" = 2026.0) AND ("Quarter" = 1.0)
```


:::
:::


Nothing has been downloaded yet. The filter is encoded as a `WHERE` clause that DuckDB will push down to the Parquet row-group level: any chunk whose min/max statistics show it contains no NS-IBTS 2026 Q1 rows is skipped entirely.

### Building up a query step by step

You can chain verbs freely. Each one adds to the SQL plan:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
q <- hl |>
  dr_add_length_cm() |>
  filter(
    Survey  == "NS-IBTS",
    Quarter == 1,
    !is.na(LengthCode)
  ) |>
  select(.id, Survey, Year, aphia, length_cm, NumberAtLength, SubsamplingFactor)

q |> show_query()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
<SQL>
SELECT
  ".id",
  Survey,
  "Year",
  aphia,
  length_cm,
  NumberAtLength,
  SubsamplingFactor
FROM (
  SELECT
    *,
    CASE
WHEN (LengthCode = '-9') THEN NULL
WHEN (LengthCode IN ('.', '0')) THEN (LengthClass / 10.0)
WHEN (LengthCode IN ('1', '2', '5')) THEN LengthClass
ELSE NULL
END AS length_cm
  FROM oiqkicywxxllxon
) AS q01
WHERE (Survey = 'NS-IBTS') AND ("Quarter" = 1.0) AND (NOT((LengthCode IS NULL)))
```


:::
:::


The `CASE WHEN` block that `dr_add_length_cm()` adds internally is translated to SQL and included in the same query. No intermediate data frame is created.

### `collect()` — the moment data moves

Only when you call `collect()` does DuckDB actually read data from the Parquet file and return it as a plain R data frame:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
system.time(
  data <- q |> collect()
)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   user  system elapsed 
  0.358   0.043   0.275 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
nrow(data)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 2301995
```


:::
:::


DuckDB reads only the columns used in the query, skips row-groups that can't match the filter, decompresses what remains, and returns the result. A query that touches a few percent of the 14-million-row file completes in under a second even over an HTTP connection.

### Joins stay lazy too

You can join multiple connections before collecting. The entire join is pushed down to DuckDB:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hh <- dr_con("HH")
sp <- dr_con("species")

q <- hl |>
  inner_join(
    hh |> select(.id, HaulValidity, DataType, HaulDuration),
    by = ".id"
  ) |>
  inner_join(sp, by = "aphia") |>
  dr_add_length_cm() |>
  dr_add_n_and_cpue() |>
  filter(
    HaulValidity == "V",
    latin        == "Gadus morhua",
    Survey       %in% c("NS-IBTS", "SCOWCGFS"),
    Quarter      == 1
  ) |>
  select(.id, Survey, Year, latin, length_cm, n_haul, n_hour)

q |> show_query()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
<SQL>
SELECT
  ".id",
  Survey,
  "Year",
  latin,
  length_cm,
  n_haul,
  (n_haul / HaulDuration) * 60.0 AS n_hour
FROM (
  SELECT
    *,
    CASE
WHEN (DataType = 'C') THEN (((NumberAtLength * SubsamplingFactor) * HaulDuration) / 60.0)
WHEN (DataType = 'R') THEN (NumberAtLength * COALESCE(SubsamplingFactor, 1.0))
WHEN (DataType = 'P') THEN (NumberAtLength * SubsamplingFactor)
WHEN (DataType = 'S') THEN (NumberAtLength * SubsamplingFactor)
WHEN (DataType = '-9') THEN NULL
WHEN ((DataType IS NULL)) THEN NULL
ELSE NULL
END AS n_haul
  FROM (
    SELECT
      *,
      CASE
WHEN (LengthCode = '.') THEN 0.1
WHEN (LengthCode = '0') THEN 0.5
WHEN (LengthCode = '1') THEN 1.0
WHEN (LengthCode = '2') THEN 2.0
WHEN (LengthCode = '5') THEN 5.0
WHEN ((LengthCode IS NULL)) THEN NULL
END AS accuracy
    FROM (
      SELECT
        *,
        CASE
WHEN (LengthCode = '-9') THEN NULL
WHEN (LengthCode IN ('.', '0')) THEN (LengthClass / 10.0)
WHEN (LengthCode IN ('1', '2', '5')) THEN LengthClass
ELSE NULL
END AS length_cm
      FROM (
        SELECT
          oiqkicywxxllxon.*,
          HaulValidity,
          DataType,
          HaulDuration,
          latin,
          species
        FROM oiqkicywxxllxon
        INNER JOIN dlegadnudtwcdnd
          ON (oiqkicywxxllxon.".id" = dlegadnudtwcdnd.".id")
        INNER JOIN dooxrtkloyvzxqu
          ON (oiqkicywxxllxon.aphia = dooxrtkloyvzxqu.aphia)
      ) AS q01
    ) AS q01
  ) AS q01
) AS q01
WHERE
  (HaulValidity = 'V') AND
  (latin = 'Gadus morhua') AND
  (Survey IN ('NS-IBTS', 'SCOWCGFS')) AND
  ("Quarter" = 1.0)
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
system.time(
  cod <- q |> collect()
)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   user  system elapsed 
  0.317   0.041   0.514 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
glimpse(cod)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Rows: 152,918
Columns: 7
$ .id       <chr> "NS-IBTS:1978:1:DK:26SA:HT:5:5", "NS-IBTS:1978:1:DK:26SA:HT:…
$ Survey    <chr> "NS-IBTS", "NS-IBTS", "NS-IBTS", "NS-IBTS", "NS-IBTS", "NS-I…
$ Year      <dbl> 1978, 1978, 1978, 1978, 1978, 1978, 1978, 1978, 1978, 1978, …
$ latin     <chr> "Gadus morhua", "Gadus morhua", "Gadus morhua", "Gadus morhu…
$ length_cm <dbl> 15, 18, 32, 14, 20, 18, 19, 20, 48, 12, 13, 15, 16, 17, 18, …
$ n_haul    <dbl> 1, 2, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 2, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 1, …
$ n_hour    <dbl> 2, 4, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 4, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 2, …
```


:::
:::


Three Parquet files joined, filtered, and computed — all in DuckDB, before a single row enters R memory.

---

## Skipping dbplyr: plain SQL

Everything shown so far has used `{dplyr}` verbs with `{dbplyr}` translating them to SQL behind the scenes. Sometimes you want to write SQL directly — for a complex query that is cleaner in SQL, for debugging, or simply because you are more comfortable with it. There are two levels at which you can do this: inside R via DBI, and outside R entirely.

### Within R

`dbplyr::remote_con()` exposes the underlying DBI connection from any `dr_con()` object. DuckDB's `read_parquet()` function lets you reference a Parquet file by URL directly in SQL, without needing to know the internal view name:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
con <- dbplyr::remote_con(dr_con("HL"))

DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT   Survey, Year, COUNT(*) AS n_hauls
  FROM     read_parquet('https://heima.hafro.is/~einarhj/datras/HL.parquet')
  WHERE    Quarter = 1
  GROUP BY Survey, Year
  ORDER BY Survey, Year
  LIMIT    12
")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   Survey Year n_hauls
1    BITS 1991   16681
2    BITS 1992   14848
3    BITS 1993   17160
4    BITS 1994   18438
5    BITS 1995   20468
6    BITS 1996   23012
7    BITS 1997   19184
8    BITS 1998   20551
9    BITS 1999   25741
10   BITS 2000   25739
11   BITS 2001   23060
12   BITS 2002   22082
```


:::
:::


You can also open a fresh DuckDB connection with no `{obus}` involvement at all:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
con <- DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb())

DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT   Survey,
           SUM(NumberAtLength * SubsamplingFactor) AS n_total
  FROM     read_parquet('https://heima.hafro.is/~einarhj/datras/HL.parquet')
  WHERE    aphia = 126436
  GROUP BY Survey
  ORDER BY n_total DESC
")

DBI::dbDisconnect(con)
```
:::


A useful middle path: pass a SQL string to `dplyr::tbl()` to get back a lazy table you can continue piping `{dplyr}` verbs onto:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
tbl(con, sql("
  SELECT .id, aphia, LengthClass, LengthCode, NumberAtLength, SubsamplingFactor
  FROM   read_parquet('https://heima.hafro.is/~einarhj/datras/HL.parquet')
  WHERE  Survey = 'NS-IBTS' AND Year = 2024 AND Quarter = 1
")) |>
  dr_add_length_cm() |>
  filter(length_cm > 40) |>
  collect()
```
:::


### The DuckDB CLI — no R required

DuckDB ships a standalone command-line executable. The Parquet files are just URLs; no R or Python installation is needed:

```bash
duckdb -c "
SELECT   Survey, Year, COUNT(*) AS n_hauls
FROM     read_parquet('https://heima.hafro.is/~einarhj/datras/HL.parquet')
WHERE    Quarter = 1
GROUP BY Survey, Year
ORDER BY Survey, Year
LIMIT    12
"
```

For longer sessions, start the interactive shell:

```bash
duckdb

D SELECT Survey, COUNT(*) AS n
  FROM read_parquet('https://heima.hafro.is/~einarhj/datras/HL.parquet')
  GROUP BY Survey ORDER BY n DESC;
```

### Python

```python
import duckdb
con = duckdb.connect()
con.execute("""
    SELECT   Survey, Year, COUNT(*) AS n_hauls
    FROM     read_parquet('https://heima.hafro.is/~einarhj/datras/HL.parquet')
    WHERE    Quarter = 1
    GROUP BY Survey, Year
    ORDER BY n_hauls DESC
    LIMIT    12
""").df()
```

In all three cases — R/DBI, CLI, Python — `read_parquet()` is doing the same work: DuckDB reads the file footer, applies the `WHERE` filter at the chunk level, and returns only the rows and columns the query needs.

---

## Same code, three backends

The point stated earlier bears a concrete demonstration. Here is a function that computes total cod catch by survey, year, and quarter:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summarise_cod <- function(hl_input, hh_input) {
  hl_input |>
    inner_join(hh_input |> select(.id, HaulValidity, DataType, HaulDuration),
               by = ".id") |>
    dr_add_length_cm() |>
    dr_add_n_and_cpue() |>
    filter(HaulValidity == "V", aphia == 126436L) |>
    group_by(Survey, Year, Quarter) |>
    summarise(n_haul_total = sum(n_haul, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
}
```
:::


Call it with **remote DuckDB connections** — only the matching rows ever leave the server:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summarise_cod(dr_con("HL"), dr_con("HH")) |> collect()
```
:::


Call it with **local DuckDB connections** — same laziness, files on your own disk:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summarise_cod(
  duckdbfs::open_dataset("~/datras/HL.parquet"),
  duckdbfs::open_dataset("~/datras/HH.parquet")
) |> collect()
```
:::


Call it with **in-memory data frames** — everything already in R:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hh_local <- dr_get("HH", surveys = "NS-IBTS", years = 2024, quarters = 1)
hl_local <- dr_get("HL", surveys = "NS-IBTS", years = 2024, quarters = 1)
summarise_cod(hl_local, hh_local)
```
:::


The function is identical in all three cases. No `if` statements, no special cases, no adapter layer. The `dr_add_*()` functions in `{obus}` are written to handle both `tbl_duckdb_connection` and ordinary `data.frame` inputs; `{dplyr}` dispatches to the right method automatically.

### When to prefer each approach

| Situation | Recommended approach |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Exploring the full dataset, filtering by survey / year / species | `dr_con()` — lazy; only your subset downloads |
| Running the same filtered subset repeatedly in one session | `dr_get()` once, then work on the data frame |
| Joining across HH + HL + species without caring about the full tables | `dr_con()` — DuckDB handles the join before collect |
| Working offline or on a slow connection | `dr_get()` up front, save locally |
| Building a function that others will call either way | Write against `{dplyr}` — it works on both |

---

## What you never have to do

A few things that used to be standard practice with DATRAS data are now unnecessary:

**Maintain local copies.** There is no need to periodically download and version a local `HL.csv`. The Parquet files on the server are the authoritative copy. Connect, filter, collect — and you always get the current version.

**Manage file formats.** You do not need to know whether the underlying storage is Snappy-compressed Parquet, zstd-compressed Parquet, or anything else. DuckDB handles the format; you handle the dplyr.

**Write SQL.** `{dbplyr}` writes it for you. `show_query()` is there if you are curious, not because you need to worry about it.

---

## Summary

```
Parquet file on server
        │
        │  (dr_con reads only the footer — nearly instant)
        ▼
DuckDB connection in R
        │
        │  (dplyr verbs build an SQL plan — no data moves yet)
        ▼
Lazy query (tbl_duckdb_connection)
        │
        │  (collect() executes the query — only matching rows and columns travel)
        ▼
Plain R data frame
```

The three technologies — Parquet, DuckDB, dbplyr — are independently useful, but together they give you interactive performance on a 14-million-row dataset, with no local storage, no SQL, and code that works identically on an in-memory data frame the moment you need to switch.
